Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered throughout this course. I'd usually try to listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around my apartment, since I learn best by hearing things. I aimed for about 10 hours a week, but it rarely happened in any organized way. I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could find a quiet moment, often late at night or early morning before my day got busy.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was happening in my life. I wanted to be more consistent, but deadlines and other responsibilities kept pulling me in different directions. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd try to catch up all at once when something was due. It wasn't ideal, but I kept trying to make it work somehow.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered there was something due or when I wanted to watch the lecture videos again. Sometimes I'd get notifications about discussion posts and that would remind me to check in. I also logged in just to see what I might have missed, especially after being away for a few days. The discussions with other students actually pulled me back more than I expected.
Q4: There was a period in the middle of the semester where checking the LMS became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, make coffee, and open the course page to see if there were new discussion posts. I really enjoyed the peer interactions, so that became my anchor. But then things got harder and that routine slipped away, even though I kept trying to rebuild it.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me because I could replay them and really absorb the information by listening. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly helpful because hearing different perspectives from my peers helped concepts click. I spent a lot of time on the videos, probably over three hours total, because I could pause and relisten to parts I didn't catch the first time.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the reading materials and some of the written resources. I just don't absorb information as well when I'm reading compared to listening. I also delayed looking at assignment guidelines until the last minute, which probably hurt me. Some of the supplementary PDFs sat unopened because I kept telling myself I'd get to them later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually hear myself understanding something new. If I could listen to a lecture and then explain the concept out loud to myself, that felt productive. The discussions also felt valuable because I was connecting with real people and seeing how they approached problems. Anything that felt passive or like busy work, I struggled to prioritize.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. When I was already behind on required work, optional stuff just fell off my radar completely. But when I did have time, I actually enjoyed some of the optional discussions because there was less pressure. Required activities carried this weight of anxiety that made them harder to start, even when they weren't actually more difficult.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded almost all the audio files and lecture recordings to my phone so I could listen while commuting or doing chores. I'd replay them multiple times without being connected to the LMS at all. I also took handwritten notes while listening, which helped me process information better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my computer entirely.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't have shown it. I'd be walking around my neighborhood listening to a downloaded lecture, pausing to think through concepts. Or I'd be in the shower mentally reviewing what I'd learned, trying to explain it to myself. I also spent time talking through ideas with a friend who was in a different program, and that helped me understand things better.
Q11: Definitely. I would download a lecture, watch it once on the LMS, and then listen to it maybe three or four more times offline over the next few days. The LMS only showed that one initial view, but I was actually engaging with that content repeatedly. This was my main study strategy, and it worked well for understanding the material, even if it didn't show up in any metrics.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard for me. I had some personal stuff going on and my motivation just tanked, even though I wasn't feeling particularly anxious about it. I kept logging in and watching videos, but I wasn't completing assignments on time. It felt like I was treading water, doing enough to stay connected but not enough to actually succeed.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. I'd log in intending to do work, but I'd end up just browsing through materials without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS use too. Some weeks I had to choose between work obligations and schoolwork, and school often lost. The confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections entirely sometimes.
Q14: Yes, this happened more than I'd like to admit. I'd have the lecture playing but my mind would be somewhere else completely. I'd scroll through the discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Looking back, I can see that I was present but not actually learning during those times. It was like my body was doing student things but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was that I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. Sometimes I'd avoid an assignment because I wasn't confident I understood the material well enough. The exam especially scared me, so I kept putting off studying for it until it was too late to really prepare properly.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of activity for me. I'd be relatively quiet on the LMS for days, then suddenly I'd be logging in multiple times trying to figure out what I needed to submit. Unfortunately, I missed a lot of deadlines anyway because my bursts weren't early enough. The pressure of deadlines sometimes paralyzed me instead of motivating me.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect my engagement, but I often didn't check feedback promptly. If I got encouraging comments, I'd feel more motivated to participate in discussions. But sometimes feedback came after I'd already moved on mentally, so it didn't have the impact it could have. I wish I'd been better about checking and using feedback throughout the course.
Q18: The structure of the course was somewhat flexible, which was both good and bad for me. I appreciated being able to access materials on my own time, but I also needed more structure than I gave myself. The discussion requirements actually helped because they gave me regular reasons to engage with peers. Without those, I might have been even less connected to the course.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. But the heavy reliance on written materials for some topics made those sections harder. I also found that when assignment instructions were complicated, I'd avoid starting them. Clearer, shorter instructions would have helped me jump in faster instead of procrastinating.
Q20: I think I might be an example of this, actually. I was logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions, but my assignment completion was really low. For me, it was about understanding versus producing. I could engage with learning materials all day, but actually creating and submitting work felt like a completely different skill that I struggled with. The anxiety of being evaluated made submitting work harder than just learning.
Q21: That doesn't really match my experience since I had high LMS activity but still failed. But I can see how it could happen for others. Some people might be really efficient, getting what they need quickly and then studying effectively offline. Or they might have strong prior knowledge and not need as much engagement with course materials. Everyone's learning process is different.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns of when someone is active and what resources they're clicking on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm actually absorbing information or just letting a video play in the background. They also miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers outside the platform, and the mental processing that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, making connections, and caring about understanding it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through things. For me, engagement feels like when I'm listening to a lecture and I pause to think, 'Oh, that connects to something else I learned.' It's active mental participation, not passive consumption. Real engagement should lead to growth, not just activity.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how hard I was trying to make this work despite everything. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while walking, or the mindfulness exercises I did to manage my stress. It wouldn't show that I genuinely cared about learning even when my grades didn't reflect it. My resilience and effort don't translate into clicks and timestamps, but they were real and they mattered to me.
